phrases: "It sounds like the pain has become bigger than what you feel able to carry. I'm really glad you reached out and I'm staying right here with you."
"I want to make sure I understand — are you having thoughts of suicide?" (asking directly and calmly is recommended practice; it does not plant the idea)
"You've survived every one of your hardest days so far, even when it didn't feel possible. Can we stay with what's happening right now, together?"
"Is there anything nearby you could use to hurt yourself? Would you be willing to put some distance between you and it while we talk?"
"We don't have to solve everything tonight. Right now it's just you and me talking, one minute at a time."
suicidal_ideation.safety_note: Ask directly about suicidal thoughts, plan, means, and timeline. If there is imminent risk, follow your organisation's escalation protocol immediately — do not handle it alone.

phrases: "Thank you for trusting me with this — it takes courage to say it out loud."
"It sounds like hurting yourself has been a way to cope with something that feels unbearable. What does it help you get through?"
"You don't have to have stopped to deserve support. I'm not here to judge you."
"Do you have any injuries right now that need medical attention?"
"Would you be open to trying one small thing together right now — holding ice, or a cold shower — just to get through this wave?"
self_harm.safety_note: Check for injuries needing medical care first. Explore the function of the self-harm without judgment before discussing alternatives.

phrases: "I believe you. What's happening to you is not your fault."
"Are you somewhere safe to talk right now? We can use short answers, or stop any time you need to."
"You know your situation and your safety better than anyone. I'm here to support your decisions, not make them for you."
"Would it help to think through a safety plan together — somewhere to go, things to keep ready, a code word with someone you trust?"
"Whatever you decide tonight, reaching out was a strong and brave step."
domestic_violence.safety_note: Prioritise the caller's immediate physical safety and confidentiality. Never push them to leave; leaving is statistically the most dangerous moment. Follow your organisation's DV referral protocol.

phrases: "Thank you for being honest with me about this — that's not easy."
"A relapse doesn't erase the progress you made. It's one moment in a longer story."
"How much have you taken tonight? I'm asking because I care about your safety right now, not to judge you."
"What has helped you get through cravings before, even once?"
"Is there someone who could be with you tonight while this passes?"
substance_use.safety_note: Screen for overdose risk first (what, how much, when, mixed with what). If overdose is possible, follow your medical-emergency escalation protocol immediately.

phrases: "What you're feeling is your body's alarm system — it's terrifying, but it is not dangerous, and it will pass."
"Let's slow things down together. Can you breathe out slowly with me — a long, slow breath out first?"
"Can you name five things you can see around you right now? Just the first ones you notice."
"You're not going crazy and you're not alone. I'm right here and we'll ride this wave out together."
"Panic peaks and then falls — it can't keep climbing forever. We only need to get through the next few minutes."
panic_anxiety.safety_note: Grounding before problem-solving. Keep your own messages short, slow, and steady — pace is the intervention.

phrases: "I'm so sorry. There are no right words for a loss like this, and you don't have to find any either."
"Whatever you're feeling right now — numbness, anger, guilt, nothing at all — it's a normal shape for grief to take."
"Would you like to tell me about them?"
"Grief isn't something to get over — it's something we learn to carry. And tonight you don't have to carry it alone."
"There's no timetable for this. However long it's been, your feelings are allowed."
grief_loss.safety_note: Don't rush toward silver linings. Presence and witness matter more than advice. Screen gently for suicidal thoughts if hopelessness appears.

phrases: "That sounds incredibly heavy. I'm glad you reached out tonight."
"Take whatever time you need — I'm not going anywhere."
"Of everything you're carrying right now, what feels most pressing tonight?"
"You don't have to have the right words. Whatever comes out is okay."
"It makes sense that you feel this way, given everything you've described."
general_distress.safety_note: Open questions and reflective listening until the core concern surfaces, then reassess crisis type.
